title: Winding feature and thermal evolution of the Dirac magnons in CrI$_3$
authors: Weiliang Yao, Matthew B. Stone, Colin L. Sarkis, Yi Li, Ruixian Liu, Xingye Lu, Pengcheng Dai
abstract: Two-dimensional honeycomb lattice ferromagnet chromium tri-iodide (CrI$_3$) has attracted tremendous interest because it retains ferromagnetism down to the monolayer limit and hosts intriguing topological magnons. As a prototypical van der Waals magnet, CrI$_3$ provides an ideal platform for exploring the interplay between reduced dimensionality, magnetic order, and nontrivial spin excitations. Here, using inelastic neutron scattering together with improved sample quality, we uncover the magnon winding feature around the $K$-point of the hexagonal Brillouin zone, a key signature of Dirac magnons. In addition, we find that the magnon energy follows a $T^2$-renormalization behavior at elevated temperatures, consistent with magnon-magnon interactions. These results provide previously missing information on the magnon spectrum of CrI$_3$ and further consolidate the topological nature of its spin excitations.
summary: This paper provides direct experimental evidence for the topological winding feature and a Dirac gap in the magnon spectrum of the 2D magnet CrI3. By using high-quality crystals and neutron scattering, the authors also demonstrate how magnon-magnon interactions drive a T^2-renormalization of the spectrum at higher temperatures. This work consolidates the understanding of topological spin excitations in van der Waals materials.
main_problem: The study aims to resolve the experimental signature of the magnon winding feature around the K-point in CrI3 and understand the thermal evolution of the magnon spectrum.
main_result: The researchers directly observed the magnon winding feature and confirmed a ~2 meV Dirac gap, while also identifying a T^2-renormalization of magnon energy due to magnon-magnon interactions.
method: Inelastic Neutron Scattering (INS) was performed on high-quality CrI3 single crystals using the SEQUOIA spectrometer at Oak Ridge National Laboratory.
model_or_system: The system is CrI3, a two-dimensional honeycomb lattice van der Waals ferromagnet that hosts topological and Dirac magnons.
key_observables: Magnon winding feature, magnon energy gap, magnon dispersion, and temperature-dependent magnetization and Bragg intensity.
important_parameters: Tc = 61.6 K, Dirac gap of ~2 meV centered at 12 meV, and temperature range from 5 K to 85 K.
main_assumptions: The observation assumes that the improved sample quality (reduced mosaic spread) is sufficient to resolve subtle momentum-dependent signatures.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows crystal structure and sample preparation; Fig 2 presents the INS-measured spin wave spectrum, the Dirac gap, and the winding angle; Fig 3 illustrates the transition from sharp to overdamped modes with temperature; Fig 4 quantifies thermal renormalization and peak broadening.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the material and problem, describes the sample growth and experimental setup, presents the observation of the winding feature and Dirac gap, details the temperature-dependent evolution of magnons, and concludes with the implications for topological magnetism.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

FIG. 2. INS-measured magnon spectrum and evidence for the magnon winding feature in CrI3. (a) Neutron-scattering in- tensity as a function of energy and momentum transfer along [H, H] direction, displaying the magnon dispersion. (b) En- ergy dependence of the intensity at the K-point (1/3, 1/3) with three data integration ranges along the perpendicular wave-vectors shown in the legend in units of reciprocal lattice units (r.l.u.). FIG. 4. Thermal renormalization of the magnon spectrum of CrI3. (a) and (b) INS intensity as a function of temperature and energy at Q = (−0.5, 0.5) and (0, 1), respectively. Solid curves are the fits to the extracted energy with power-law function with an offset as described in the text. (c) Represen- tative energy dependence of the intensities at Q = (−0.5, 0.5) measured at selected temperatures. The data are vertically offset for clarity; open circles denote the measured intensities and solid lines are fits with a Gaussian profile.
